results.title: E-TTS: A New Embodied Test-Time Scaling Framework for Robotic ...
results.content:
w/o action scaling and w/o reasoning scaling apply only one scaling component. Using either alone substantially degrades performance, indicating that both are necessary for embodied tasks.

w/o joint scoring evaluates reasoning and action separately. We first select the highest-scoring reasoning, then sample and score actions conditioned on it. This strategy performs poorly, as high-quality reasoning does not guarantee high-quality actions. Effective evaluation requires joint scoring (Sec. 3.2).

w/o history buffer removes historical inputs during evaluation, reducing success rate, demonstrating the importance of trajectory information.

w/o ϵ\epsilon-greedy disables exploration, resulting in lower performance and highlighting the need to balance exploration and exploitation. [...] To summarize, our main contributions are threefold:

We introduce a novel plug-and-play embodied test-time scaling framework that can be seamlessly integrated with various different vision-language-action models, enhancing their success rates without the need for additional expert data collection or retraining.

We tackle the unique challenges of the embodied domain by jointly scaling reasoning and action through history-aware, iterative refinement with vision–language verifiers, leading to significant performance improvements over conventional test-time scaling methods. [...] Test-time scaling inherently trades inference time for performance. However, our empirical results show that this trade-off is favorable. As detailed in Table 5, an optimal scaling configuration yields a 150% relative improvement in success rate with only a 46.6% increase in latency, reflecting high sampling efficiency.

results.title: Lecture 10: Embodied Reasoning and Test-time Scaling | ETH Zürich
results.content: with all of this. Reasoning is unfortunately slow. Because we are basically increasing the number of tokens generated at test time quite significantly. So open VLA which is kind of the the base model we were using in this work. Runs like it's a 7B model so it runs about um four actions per second. And the embodied reasoning version of it that we developed because it [clears throat] it had to like produce like this this full like reasoning chain runs at four seconds per action. [laughter] So it's quite different quite a difference. Like we managed to do some inference speed optimizations later on but the first ever rollouts that actually Will and Michal who were the lead authors of this work did [clears throat] on the real like WidowX robot were really painful like just to watch like [...] rewarded and checked, uh, at every step, including this this, uh, temporal grounding in the videos. And so, coming back now from language and vision to, um, embodied AI and robotics, uh, which is ultimately, uh, what we care about, um, what we observed in this work is that, um, test-time reasoning gives you large gains on high-level planning, uh, high-level planning tasks, um, like kind of like simulated, uh, um, kind of tasks, uh, [clears throat] like, uh, Alfred and so on. Um, on the hardest tasks, um, um, this approach, uh, can give you, um, three to four times, uh, better, uh, performance than, uh, supervised chain-of-thought, uh, models. Which basically means that a model, um, that has learned to reason, uh, through reinforcement learning, generalizes to hard problems in a way that a [...] that without collecting any additional robot data for the same amount of robot teleop data you already have collected you can improve your your models generalization capabilities by around 30% by applying this embodied reasoning technique. And as a side benefit beyond generalization performance it also gives you better interpretability and interactiveness because now like we these, uh, reasoning steps are, like, human-readable, you can see, like, where it's predicting the bounding boxes and so forth. Uh, at test time, you can also see, um, like, if the robot is predicting a wrong plan and edit this reasoning chains online, uh, and because the action tokens are conditioned on this intermediate reasoning tokens, it will naturally, um, kind of, um, self-correct itself, uh, like, if a human

results.title: [PDF] EQA-RM: A Generative Embodied Reward Model with Test-time ...
results.content:
critiques from EQA-RM en-hance interpretability and enable test-time scaling, a systematic evaluation of their fine-grained faith-fulness or their direct utility in, for example, few-shot learning for policy adaptation, was beyond the scope of this paper. [...] Test-time Scaling Configuration. To enhance EQA-RM’s evaluation robustness, our test-time scal-ing (TTS) strategy involves sampling K diverse evaluative reasoning paths from a given EQA tra-jectory. This is facilitated by a temperature setting of 0.8 and top-p sampling with p = 0.9. We ex-plore K values of 1 (no TTS), 2, 4, 8, 16, and 32.
These K assessments are aggregated using either Majority Voting or Averaging Rewards for continu-ous scores. This TTS approach enables EQA-RM to synthesize multiple perspectives, aiming for a more reliable and comprehensive trajectory assessment by mitigating single-inference pass limitations. [...] While the base EQA-RM’s accuracy is lower than leading VLM-as-a-Judge models such as GPT-4o and Gemini-2.5-Flash, it provides more precise re-ward signals, evidenced by its considerably lower RMSE. Crucially, with test-time scaling (K=32), EQA-RM’s accuracy significantly increases to an overall score of approximately 61.9%, surpass-ing these top VLM judges and achieving the best RMSE. This positions the scaled EQA-RM as the top-performing model on our benchmark.
5.3 Test-time Scalability The benefits of test-time scaling for EQA-RM are demonstrated in Figure 3. As the number of sam-pled critiques (K) increases from 1 to 32, EQA-RM exhibits a strong and consistent improvement in per-formance. Overall accuracy sees substantial gains.
